speaker: interviewer
text: The date is October 2, 1995. The survivor's name is Mrs. Ethel Katz. She was born Ethel Gojcherman. That's G-O-J-C-H-E-R-M-A-N. I am the interviewer. My name is Richard Bassett. B-A-S-S-E-T-T. We are in Toronto, Canada, and the interview will be held in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: The date is October 2, 1995. The survivor's name is Mrs. Ethel Katz. She was born Ethel Gojcherman. That's G-O-J-C-H-E-R-M-A-N. I am the interviewer. My name is Richard Bassett-- B-A-S-S-E-T-T. We are in Toronto, Canada, and the interview will be held in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ethel Gojcherman.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your maiden name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Gojcherman, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-O-I-C-H-E-R-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your married name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Katz. K-A-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Ostrog-- this is Poland-- in the 22nd of November, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Ostrog, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-S-T-R-O-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hersh.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ita.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was Feldman.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I remember, he hadn't been having a steady job. He was a sick man, but he helped out in the business suite doing some accounting for them. He worked at home. He didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a bookkeeper type of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. That's all you remember him doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a lot of brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was having three brothers and four sisters. The oldest was from another mother-- my mother's sister. She passed away when the second child just was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my grandfather liked very much my father, and he didn't want him to marry another woman. So he started to talk into my mother to marry him. She was very young. She must have been about 16-years-old though.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she got married, and she got already two children, two girls. The oldest was about three years old, and the youngest was just one. The oldest's name was Frieda and the younger, Hanna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then your, your other brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then it was my oldest brother-- three brothers. The oldest was Moshe, [? Yosef, ?] and [? Haskel. ?] And then I was having two sisters, plus me-- [? Faygi, ?] [? Tzirl, ?] and Ethel-- me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the baby, yeah. Actually, our family emigrated to Ostrog from Lutsk Gubernia. It's like a province, you know? The name of this city-- it was a little city-- [NON-ENGLISH]. It was the name. The reason-- I don't know why they emigrated, because all the family was there. And like, my mother's sisters, they was having land, and they worked on the land, plus the stock they was having. It was German people living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they left [NON-ENGLISH] and they came to Ostrog.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Ostrog. They came with all the children. Just me, I was born in Ostrog. And it was after the first World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about where you lived? The address, the actual building? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLEARS THROAT] The, the address was [NON-ENGLISH], the name of the street. The number, I don't remember-- 69, about that. I don't remember. It was a nice street. It was very long, and it was ending with a bridge, a long bridge. It was having a famous, big river-- Horyn was the name of it. This river, we have been-- like Ostrog, it was Ukraine. Actually, it belonged to Poland, but it's the part or Ukraine. It used to be. And our city was right on the border. The border of the city was the river. And then it was woods, and it started Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- the actual house-- [CLEARS THROAT] Pardon me. The house that you lived in-- was it a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a house. It wasn't, there, apartments. It was a Jewish city. The population was about 10,000. And the most-- it was Jewish people. They was workers, like shoemakers, dressmakers, and stores. All kinds of stores. This was all handled by the Jewish people. From around, it was the villages. They was coming to buy stuff from the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you actually live, though? Describe it to me. How many rooms? What was it like inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't own our own house. We rented a house. So we had been in, in two houses, since I remember, all the time we lived there. It was like we was having a-- like a living/dining room, and a bedroom-- two bedrooms, and a kitchen. This is all it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we, the three girls, slept in one room. The father, mother in the, in the bedroom. And the boys slept on couches.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Living/dining room, you know? It wasn't luxury like here, that's for sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we haven't been deprived. We-- I have to tell you all the story about how we, we got there. When we came to Ostrog-- this is what I heard from my parents-- my oldest sister, [? Hanna, ?] from the first marriage-- she was then about 15, 16 years old. Maybe a bit older, maybe 18. And she was a beautiful girl. You have seen her in-- on the picture. And she met a boy, and he fell in love with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was-- he was a rich man. He was buying cattle and selling it to the, to the army, to the butchers, you know? So thanks to her-- he married her. And thanks to her, he took in all the family in the business.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my three brothers worked with him-- for him, worked with him-- you know? So the oldest was Moshe. And then-- and he got married from a girl not from our town, to a shadchan, you know? She was a poor girl, but from a very religious family. Her father was a soifer. Her brother-- one was a rabbi, and the other one was a shochet. But she was poor, and she was looking for, for a husband who will take her without-- how would you say-- nothing? Without a dowry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dowry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us, in English, what a shadchan, and a soifer, and a shochet is, because since we're doing the interview in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a soifer, who writes the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Torah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Torah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: A shochet is who kills the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The ritual slaughterer.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Who--

speaker: interviewer
text: A ritual slaughterer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Checks it. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: And a shadchan.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a shadchan?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We haven't got--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is a matchmaker.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. A shadchan is a matchmaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's clear now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my oldest brother got married, and they went to live with us in this house. So we gave her-- we was having one room extra-- this was the second house, not the first house. We gave them a, a little room for-- just for sleeping. And the rest, it was altogether. They lived with us about two years till they got born, the first child. And then they moved out. [? Yosef ?] wasn't married. He was engaged, and he broke up the engagement. He wasn't married anyways. He was more involved, because he was the most that was keeping all us up. We had been three girls. And you know, in the old country, girls didn't work. But my mother helped him. He was having a butcher store. And those have been providing-- like my younger brothers, [? Haskel ?] and [? Moshe, ?] they used to go be in the villages and buying the cattle. And they provided to all the butchers, not only to him, the cattle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there, I--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to stop for a moment--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --to get my thoughts.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] You're telling us about your brother-in-law and his butcher business, and, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was rich and so, wanting to help the whole family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He helped the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: My older sister, her husband, his name was Chaim, and their last name was Bieber. They have-- he provided a very good living for her. They was having a beautiful house, and they was having even a maid helping them, you know? They got four children. The first one was a girl, and her name was Gina. And then was [? Mendel, ?] and Moshe, and [INAUDIBLE]. No, not [INAUDIBLE]-- I forgot. It will come to me later. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What else you want to know? Tell me about your home life. Were you religious? What language did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- all the Jewish people speak in Jewish. This was the language in every house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever use Polish or Ukrainian, ever?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We used, because we went to Polish schools, the Ukrainian schools. We used it only when we went to school. Otherwise, we used the Yiddish. Just Jewish, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: But were you religious at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My father was very, very religious. Actually, all the people have been religious there in those days. It's after, when Russia was taking over, then they made a big mishmash. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, who were your friends? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I was having very good friend. Two girls, they did live close to us-- which, I spent a lot of time in their house, because they was having the mother at home. My mother used to be in the store with my brother, you know, helping him out. So I didn't have much to talk to. My sisters, you know, they were always busy, this and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to spend a lot of time with them. And they have been very, very religious, those people. They owned a store, and I went to school with them. Did you have any non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exactly. At school, maybe?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. No. It's-- where I went, it was the most Jewish kids. And this school-- the name of the school was Kazimierz after a king, a Polish king. Kazimierz Wielki. Do you ever remember any anti-Semitic incidents happening as you were growing up? I don't mean in 1939, but in the '20s, and-- let's say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the evenings, we used to be afraid to go out, like in the dark places.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would happen? What could happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they, they, they didn't do any big harms, but they have been drunk, and you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Gentiles. So we avoided them. That's all. But like I'm saying, we didn't have too many Christian people in our city. It was a strictly Yiddish, Jewish place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- do you know much about your mother's and father's family, their brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's-- my father's side-- yeah. He was having one sister, who lived in [NON-ENGLISH] too. She was having, I think, to some site. I don't remember them, because it's not where I lived. I visited them, in all my life, once. But my mother was having two sisters. One was [? Tzirl. ?] And their name was Feldman. No, I'm sorry. I, I have been mistaken. Feldman was my grandfather's name. Zuberman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zulerman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zuberman. Zuberman. And then was the Shprintze. It was the youngest. My mother was the second. Shprintze's name, I don't remember. I don't recall it at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on your father's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't have family there. Maybe because of that, he-- [LAUGHS] He left after the war, or before the war, he left to Ostrog.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My grandfather's name was Moshe. And my grandmother must have been [? Faygi, ?] because my sister was after her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Moshe and [? Faygi ?] are on your father's or your mother's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's. My father's didn't have any--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't know-- but you don't know their names either?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not at all. No. All the family, it was my mother's family living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I belonged to Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of activities did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to go there, spend the time. We were serving. They was talking about Israel, to help them, to do-- was having lots of discussions. And once a week, we was having dances. Just kept us going, be up and busy. We liked to be active.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go out of town for any reason, outside of Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I have been--

speaker: interviewer
text: To the neighboring towns, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: People didn't travel then. Very seldom. Like I'm saying, to this town from my mother's side, it's only once I went to visit them. And then you have to save up for a long time to get the tickets to get there. So it wasn't easy. There wasn't any money for traveling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me anything else about Ostrog-- the synagogues, there? The--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were having the most beautiful synagogue. It was named [NON-ENGLISH] after a big rabbi. And it was a big shul. It's like-- to tell you how is was, the structure-- it was very high, and then it was having, like, three stories. Little shuls inside the big shul. In this little shuls, in one of the little-- my father, he used to belong there. They were serving a counter there, with a choir. It was a very famous shul. Especially in, in the first war. This. I remember it. My parents was telling me the story. In the first war, World War, when they starting to shoot the bomb, it was hanging in the shul, and it isn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Explode.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Explode. And who was coming to visit this from overseas-- people, they always were showing this place. And it was an underground that was taking you-- that people have been hiding in the first war, World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taking you all the length of the city, the underground. What else can I tell you? It was many shuls, small ones. But this was the biggest and the nicest. Was there a main industry, there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it, it-- it was like industry, not much. It was like a [NON-ENGLISH] you know, that they was wood-cutting. Others, I don't really recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you generally say that you had a happy childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say, yes. We haven't been rich, but we haven't been poor, but I knew that I was having.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not a lot of family life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very quiet life, a [NON-ENGLISH] life-- you know about it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Mhm. So when did things start to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1939. The Russian-- the Russian was taking over. Like Poland, it was divided. It was the Germans was taking-- starting to go in on one side. And then the Russians started to go in the other side. We have been close to the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one night, we, we went to sleep with the Polish people in the morning. We, we woke up, it was all the Russian people there. The whole army was there. So they started to make all those changes. They took away from the stores all the goods. They-- it was very hard with the food. You couldn't get any food. You have to stay in lines for hours and hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was bad, in one way. And the other way, like cultural things, it wasn't bad, because they started to educate people. They wanted to make lots of schools. We was having cultural clubs, like, they were showing films, and they-- and they started to make schools for all who never went to school. There was lots of people who never went to school, because they had to work. They had to make a living. They didn't have time to go to school. And then they started to open schools. They, they started to take in people who wants to take courses to teach. They needed badly teachers, because not only in the city, but in the villages, they sent-- they wanted to send teachers there, too. So I applied, and they took me in. And I went only for one year, and they sent me right away to teach.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in 1939 to 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939 till 1940. And then I have been teaching in the summertime. They sent us to upgrade our-- you know, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I couldn't-- we did have the seminars at our city, but they didn't let me. They were saying, you have to go to Rovno. It was a big city there. The university is there. You have to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there the first year. The second year-- you know, I haven't seen my parents. This was the first time in my life I haven't seen them for almost two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was begging them. I want to go to my city, and I can continue my studies there. So they let me go, and this was in 1941. I went only a couple of times. I got a chance to go, and this was in June, the 31st about when it started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to be in touch with your parents in any way throughout those two years? Like, by letter or telephone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah. No telephone, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No telegraph? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Letters,

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Letters, just by letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of life did you lead where you were-- while you were taking your course?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the evenings, I went. In the daytime, I have been free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. yes. It was like a rule. Like I'm saying, it was a Jewish city. Saturday, all the stores have been closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I-- not-- I don't mean in Ostrog, now. I mean, wherever you were when you were taking your courses, when you were outside of Ostrog.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I could keep up Shabbos. It was two days, you have to keep up-- Shabbos and Sunday. It was everything closed. And Shabbos was everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Russians didn't interfere with you keeping Shabbos or going to shul or whatever?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the Russians came up, yes. They interfered. I'm talking about when we had been in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And that's what I'm asking you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were learning, as-- when you were taking your course as a teacher, and you were away from home for two years, were you able to keep Shabbos then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have been alone. They didn't-- they didn't teach in Shabbos and Sunday, anyways. We didn't have courses. It's two days, it wasn't any courses. So who knew if I kept it up or not. I have been the only one. I-- I have done what I wanted to do, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not, I have to go to the shul or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after 1941, when you came back to Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I met my husband. We have been-- not engaged-- but we have been going together. We kept in touch when I have been away in letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's from the same street-- not only city-- from the same street.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew each other since you were kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. We hadn't been so close when this started, but before the war, about three years or four years, we started to go, like, steady, like they saying here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I have been away, we just are sending letters from each other. And when I came, he used to take me to school and pick me up, you know? And one night, we went there, came home, and that's it. And it started, the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the Russian people was going to all the houses, saying, run away, run away. Go to the woods. We were heading like the river, I told you, and then the woods. And like, up street was a long, long street, and ended with a bridge. So you passed the bridge, there was the woods. We went to the woods. Yeah. I didn't tell you this story. When they started to say, all the young people has to go away. So my sister, my older sister, [? Tzirl ?] was having a boyfriend. And my older sister, [? Faygi, ?] was married already. They was having a child, about three years old. So they came up to my mother's place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were saying, we're going away, to me. They were saying, you want to go with us? You can go with us. I was saying, I just got home. What about the parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't want to go, but I persuade them to go. I was saying, if you're not going, I'm not going either. So they went. They went. And my older sister-- which one got married, I told you, and got four kids. They have been rich. [LAUGHS] They was having their own wagon and horses. So we put on the wagon and the horses just the kids and my parents. And we have been, for awhile, but later on, we couldn't be there, because it wasn't space. So we went after the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked behind it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Uh Huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We came to the woods, and we was thinking, like I'm saying-- they were saying, go to the woods. Tomorrow, you'll be back. We never have seen the house back. We closed the houses. We only got the keys, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: While we have been sitting in the woods, all our city was bombed. And we have been so scared and cold. And I remember, like now, my father was having a bottle of whiskey in the pocket, you know? He never drank, but how the bottle of whiskey appeared in his pocket, I don't know. And he--
